content.text: Every simulator is a compressed model of physics. It omits soft-body deformation, precise contact mechanics, sensor bias, and the millisecond-scale delays introduced by real motor drivers. A policy trained entirely inside this compressed world learns to exploit the model's assumptions, so when it meets real hardware—with its stiction, IMU drift, cable compliance, and thermal nonlinearity—performance degrades sharply. This divergence is the reality gap, and closing it is the central engineering challenge of sim-to-real transfer in robotics.

attrs.alt: Reality Gap: Sources and Mitigation Strategies — Simulation (Ideal) on the left, Real World on the right, the gap in the centre, and four mitigation strategy boxes below converging to a Closed Reality Gap output.

content.text: What Is the Reality Gap?

The reality gap is the measurable difference between the state transition function modelled by a physics simulator and the true transition function of the physical system. Formally, if f_sim(s, a) is the simulator's predicted next state and f_real(s, a) is what actually happens, the gap is the norm ||f_real(s, a) - f_sim(s, a)|| averaged over the state-action distribution induced by a trained policy. Even small per-step errors compound over a trajectory: a 1 % state error per timestep produces roughly a 63 % error after 100 steps. The gap is not a flaw in any particular simulator—it is inherent to the trade-off between simulation speed and physical fidelity.
Rigid-body simulators assume infinite joint stiffness, which overestimates peak forces
LCP contact solvers use simplified Coulomb friction with a finite set of friction directions
Simulator time steps (2–10 ms) miss sub-millisecond transients in hard contacts
Default mesh colliders replace smooth surfaces with coarse polyhedra
Gravity is applied uniformly; local gravitational variation is ignored
Aerodynamic drag and fluid interaction are absent unless explicitly modelled
Unmodeled Physics

Contact dynamics are the largest single contributor to the reality gap for manipulation and legged locomotion tasks. Real contacts involve asperity deformation, adhesion, and viscoelastic responses that are far too expensive to simulate at RL training speed. Rigid-body solvers resolve penetration through constraint impulses, which produces velocity discontinuities that do not appear in real materials. Cable-driven robot hands introduce tendon compliance: a commanded joint angle is reached only asymptotically through a spring-damper chain, whereas the simulator teleports the joint to the exact commanded position. Flexible link deformation is entirely absent in most simulator pipelines. For legged robots, ground deformation under foot impact absorbs energy that the simulator incorrectly reflects back into the body.
Viscoelastic contact: real materials deform under load, absorbing energy
Tendon routing paths shift during motion, coupling joint degrees of freedom
Gearbox wear changes effective gear ratio over the robot's lifetime
Base compliance in welded robot frames contributes small but accumulating vibrations
Thermal expansion changes link lengths by 10–200 micrometres during operation
Fluid coupling between moving parts in oil-lubricated gearboxes is load-dependent
Sensor Noise and Latency

Physical sensors do not provide the clean, synchronous, zero-latency observations that simulators return by default. Joint encoders introduce quantisation error—a 12-bit encoder on a 360-degree range has 0.088-degree resolution. Inertial measurement units accumulate bias over time and are sensitive to vibration: gyroscope white noise is typically 0.003–0.05 degrees/s/sqrt(Hz). RGB cameras introduce rolling shutter distortion at fast motion speeds, and depth sensors produce sparse or invalid readings at reflective surfaces. All readings pass through drivers and ROS topics with non-deterministic latency. A policy that expects synchronous state at each step will issue actions that are already out of date by the time they reach the actuator.
Joint encoder quantisation: finite bits → discrete position values
Encoder index pulse misreads produce rare but large position spikes
IMU gyro bias: 0.1–1.0 deg/s drift accumulates over seconds
IMU accelerometer noise: 100–500 µg/sqrt(Hz) translates to position error over time
Depth camera noise: structured-light systems fail at < 0.3 m and on shiny surfaces
ROS topic jitter: sensor timestamps can lag actuation by 5–20 ms unpredictably
Actuator Nonlinearity and Delay

Every real actuator chain—from commanded torque to joint motion—passes through nonlinear elements that the simulator omits. Servo motors have a dead band: commands below a minimum PWM threshold produce no motion due to static friction overcoming the electromagnetic force. Beyond that, the current-to-torque relationship is nonlinear at high currents due to magnetic saturation. Gearboxes introduce backlash (angular free-play), stiction (static friction higher than kinetic), and viscous drag that depends on temperature and lubrication state. The motor driver's internal PD control loop runs at 1–8 kHz but the policy runs at 50–500 Hz; the outer loop sees a filtered, delayed version of the command response. Total end-to-end latency from action computation to mechanical response commonly ranges from 10 to 40 ms.
Motor dead band: PWM commands below threshold produce no motion
Gear backlash: direction reversal has ~0.1–2.0 degrees of free play
Stiction: static friction coefficient 1.5–3.0x higher than kinetic
Thermal derating: motor torque constant drops 0.3–0.5 % per degree C above rated temperature
Driver saturation: current limiting clips peak torque during fast reversals
Closed-loop driver response: appears as a second-order low-pass filter to the policy
System Identification: Calibrating the Simulator

System identification (SysID) is the process of measuring real hardware behaviour and fitting simulator parameters to reproduce it. Unlike domain randomisation, which broadens the distribution the policy trains over, SysID narrows the distribution around the measured truth—a technique sometimes called grounded simulation. The standard workflow is: (1) apply a carefully designed excitation signal to the real hardware while recording the response; (2) simulate the same excitation with candidate parameters; (3) minimise a residual loss between the real and simulated trajectories using scipy.optimize, gradient descent, or Bayesian optimisation. For robot joints, chirp signals (linearly swept sinusoids) excite the system across a range of frequencies and produce well-conditioned data for fitting inertia, damping, and stiffness parameters simultaneously.
Chirp excitation: sweeps 0.5–15 Hz to excite resonances without mechanical damage
Step response: reveals first-order time constant and steady-state gain
Sine sweep: frequency domain fit via Bode plot analysis
Pseudo-random binary sequence (PRBS): broadband, low crest factor, widely used in control SysID
Gravity compensation experiments: slowly move joint through range to fit friction parameters
Box drop tests: measure contact restitution and friction from high-speed camera replay

"""
Offline joint-level system identification for a robot arm.
Excites the joint with a chirp signal and fits inertia / damping / stiffness.
"""
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import minimize
from scipy.signal import chirp


def chirp_excitation(t: np.ndarray, f0: float = 0.5, f1: float = 10.0) -> np.ndarray:
    """Chirp torque signal sweeping f0 → f1 Hz."""
    return 0.3 * chirp(t, f0=f0, f1=f1, t1=t[-1], method="linear")


def simulate_joint(params: np.ndarray, torques: np.ndarray, dt: float) -> np.ndarray:
    """Euler-integrated 1-DOF joint: I*theta_ddot + b*theta_dot + k*theta = tau."""
    I, b, k = params
    n = len(torques)
    theta = np.zeros(n)
    omega = np.zeros(n)
    for i in range(1, n):
        alpha = (torques[i - 1] - b * omega[i - 1] - k * theta[i - 1]) / I
        omega[i] = omega[i - 1] + alpha * dt
        theta[i] = theta[i - 1] + omega[i] * dt
    return theta


def sysid_objective(
    params: np.ndarray, torques: np.ndarray, theta_real: np.ndarray, dt: float
) -> float:
    if np.any(params <= 0):
        return 1e9
    theta_sim = simulate_joint(params, torques, dt)
    return float(np.mean((theta_sim - theta_real) ** 2))


dt = 0.01
t = np.arange(0, 5.0, dt)
torques = chirp_excitation(t)
# In practice: theta_real = read_encoder_bag(ros_bag_file)
theta_real = simulate_joint([0.08, 0.5, 3.0], torques, dt)  # mock ground truth

x0 = np.array([0.05, 0.3, 2.0])  # initial guess
result = minimize(
    sysid_objective,
    x0,
    args=(torques, theta_real, dt),
    method="Nelder-Mead",
    options={"xatol": 1e-5, "fatol": 1e-6, "maxiter": 10_000},
)
I_fit, b_fit, k_fit = result.x
print(f"Identified: I={I_fit:.4f}  b={b_fit:.4f}  k={k_fit:.4f}")
# → Identified: I=0.0800  b=0.5000  k=3.0000
Residual Physics Learning

Even after system identification, a residual gap remains because many physical effects are structurally absent from rigid-body simulators—they cannot be captured by tuning existing parameters. Residual physics learning addresses this by training a neural network to predict the correction term delta_f(s, a) = f_real(s, a) - f_sim(s, a). The approach decomposes the real dynamics into a known (simulator) part and an unknown (learned) part. This is sample-efficient: the neural network only needs to learn a small residual rather than the full dynamics. Gaussian processes are preferred when the real-world dataset is small (< 5000 transitions) because they provide uncertainty estimates; MLPs scale better to higher-dimensional state spaces. The trained residual network is frozen and inserted as a wrapper around the simulator during RL training, without requiring any changes to the policy architecture or training algorithm.
GP residual: uncertainty estimates allow safety filtering at deployment time
MLP residual: scales to high-dimensional humanoid state spaces (> 100 dims)
Spectral normalisation on residual MLP prevents unstable gradient flow during RL training
Separate residuals per body segment improve generalisation for multi-link robots
Residual dataset must cover the state-action distribution the policy will actually use
Periodic residual network retraining with new real rollouts keeps the correction fresh

"""
Residual dynamics wrapper.
Adds a learned correction delta_f(s, a) on top of the simulator's step output,
closing the gap between sim and real transition functions.
"""
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import gymnasium as gym


class ResidualNet(nn.Module):
    """Predicts delta_obs = obs_real_next - obs_sim_next."""
    def __init__(self, obs_dim: int, act_dim: int) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(obs_dim + act_dim, 128), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, 128), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, obs_dim),
        )

    def forward(self, obs: torch.Tensor, act: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        return self.net(torch.cat([obs, act], dim=-1))


class ResidualEnv(gym.Wrapper):
    """Wraps a sim env to apply a frozen, pretrained ResidualNet correction."""

    def __init__(self, env: gym.Env, residual_net: ResidualNet) -> None:
        super().__init__(env)
        self.residual_net = residual_net
        self.residual_net.eval()

    def step(self, action: np.ndarray):
        obs_before = self.env.unwrapped._get_obs()          # sim obs before step
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Adaptive Control

Adaptive control updates controller parameters online during deployment, without requiring a learned model of the dynamics. The Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) framework defines a reference model capturing desired closed-loop behaviour. A parameter adaptation law—typically derived from Lyapunov stability analysis—adjusts gains in real time so that the actual system output tracks the reference model output. The MIT rule is the simplest such law: it descends the gradient of the squared tracking error with respect to an adaptive gain parameter. For robotics, MRAC is most effective for tasks where the dominant uncertainty is a scalar parameter such as payload mass, which shifts the effective inertia. Recursive Least Squares (RLS) is an alternative that maintains a covariance matrix over estimated parameters, achieving faster convergence at the cost of more computation.
MRAC: gradient-descent adaptation of control gains against tracking error
RLS: optimal linear estimator with exponential forgetting for time-varying systems
Lyapunov-based adaptation: formally guarantees bounded tracking error
L1 adaptive control: adds a fast predictor loop for robustness to fast disturbances
Composite adaptation: uses prediction error and tracking error simultaneously
Parameter projection: ensures adaptation stays within physically plausible bounds

"""
MRAC (Model Reference Adaptive Control) for a 1-DOF joint with unknown inertia.
The MIT rule gradient-descends on tracking error to update the control gain.
"""
import numpy as np


class MRACController:
    """
    Reference model: I_ref * theta_ddot + b_ref * theta_dot + k_ref * theta = r
    Plant:           I_true * theta_ddot + b * theta_dot + k * theta = u
    Adaptive law:    dK_hat/dt = -gamma * e * theta
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        I_ref: float = 0.1,
        b_ref: float = 1.0,
        k_ref: float = 5.0,
        gamma: float = 2.0,
        dt: float = 0.01,
    ) -> None:
        self.I_ref = I_ref
        self.b_ref = b_ref
        self.k_ref = k_ref
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.dt = dt
        self.theta_m = 0.0
        self.omega_m = 0.0
        self.K_hat = 1.0 / I_ref  # initial gain estimate

    def _reference_step(self, r: float) -> tuple[float, float]:
        alpha_m = (r - self.b_ref * self.omega_m - self.k_ref * self.theta_m) / self.I_ref
        self.omega_m += alpha_m * self.dt
        self.theta_m += self.omega_m * self.dt
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Real-World Adaptation

Meta-learning trains a policy initialisation that can adapt to a new environment in very few gradient steps. Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) accomplishes this by optimising the initial parameters theta* so that a small number of gradient updates on task-specific data yields a high-performing adapted policy. In the sim-to-real context, the distribution of tasks is a distribution of simulator instances with different physics parameters—the same distribution used for domain randomisation. At deployment, the robot collects k episodes of real-world data (typically 5–50 trials), performs k inner-loop gradient steps to obtain adapted parameters theta', and then executes theta'. Because the meta-training objective explicitly optimises for fast adaptation, the resulting initial parameters encode a prior over robot dynamics that generalises across the sim-to-real gap. ProMP, MAML++, and RL-squared are variants that improve stability and sample efficiency over vanilla MAML.
MAML outer loop: meta-gradient minimises loss after k inner-loop steps
Inner loop: task-specific SGD/Adam updates on the current sim variant
Second-order MAML: computes Hessian-vector products; first-order (FOMAML) approximates these
ProMP: proximal meta-policy search with KL trust region on inner-loop updates
RMA (Rapid Motor Adaptation): from Chen et al. 2021, adapts a latent physics embedding online
Adaptation context: concatenate recent (obs, action) history as implicit task encoder

"""
Simplified MAML outer-loop training for robotics sim-to-real.
Meta-trains theta* across a distribution of physics-randomised environments.
"""
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.optim import SGD, Adam
from copy import deepcopy


class Policy(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, obs_dim: int, act_dim: int) -> None:
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(obs_dim, 64), nn.Tanh(),
            nn.Linear(64, 64), nn.Tanh(),
            nn.Linear(64, act_dim),
        )

    def forward(self, obs: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        return self.net(obs)


def rollout_loss(policy: Policy, env, n_steps: int = 64) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Negative cumulative reward over a short rollout."""
    obs, _ = env.reset()
    total = torch.tensor(0.0)
    for _ in range(n_steps):
        act = policy(torch.tensor(obs, dtype=torch.float32))
        obs, rew, term, trunc, _ = env.step(act.detach().numpy())
        total = total + rew
        if term or trunc:
            break
    return -total

# ... (truncated for brevity)
Grounded Simulation

Grounded simulation combines system identification and domain randomisation: rather than randomising physics parameters uniformly over a broad prior range, it centres the distribution on measured values and applies a narrower variance. If SysID identifies the real arm's inertia as 0.082 kg·m², the training distribution might be Uniform(0.07, 0.095) instead of Uniform(0.03, 0.3). This tight grounding reduces unnecessary variation while still providing robustness to manufacturing tolerances and wear. Grounded simulation is particularly effective when the SysID cost is low relative to training cost—spending two hours on hardware measurement can save days of over-randomised training. The MuJoCo XML format and IsaacGym's domain randomisation API both allow per-parameter distribution specification, making it straightforward to implement grounded distributions as code once hardware measurements are available.
Measure inertia via pendulum swings or CAD + weighing for links and payloads
Measure friction by recording torque vs. velocity at constant-speed joint motion
Measure motor delay by sending step commands and timing encoder response in rosbag
Set DR range to measured_value ± 2 * measurement_uncertainty for principled bounds
Repeat SysID after hardware changes (new end-effector, re-lubrication, replacement parts)
Log measured values alongside code for reproducibility of training runs
Injecting Noise and Delay into the Simulator

A complementary approach to closing the reality gap is augmenting the training environment with models of the imperfections that are known to exist but are hard to measure precisely. Adding Gaussian observation noise forces the policy to function under partial information, producing more robust learned representations. Adding action delay forces the policy to account for the latency between decision and execution, naturally encouraging shorter-horizon action planning and gentler motions. Sensor dropout—randomly zeroing out individual observation dimensions—simulates sensor failure and teaches the policy to fall back on redundant information. All of these wrappers are cheap to implement as Gymnasium wrappers and impose no computational overhead beyond the added Python overhead, which is negligible compared to physics simulation cost.

"""
Observation noise and action delay wrappers.
Apply both before sim training to harden the policy against
real-world sensor imperfections and motor control loop latency.
"""
import numpy as np
import gymnasium as gym
from collections import deque


class ObservationNoiseWrapper(gym.ObservationWrapper):
    """Per-dimension Gaussian noise with optional sensor dropout."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        env: gym.Env,
        noise_std: float | np.ndarray = 0.01,
        dropout_prob: float = 0.0,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__(env)
        obs_dim = env.observation_space.shape[0]
        self.noise_std = (
            np.full(obs_dim, noise_std)
            if isinstance(noise_std, (int, float))
            else np.asarray(noise_std)
        )
        self.dropout_prob = dropout_prob

    def observation(self, obs: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        noisy = obs + np.random.randn(len(obs)) * self.noise_std
        if self.dropout_prob > 0.0:
            mask = np.random.rand(len(obs)) > self.dropout_prob
            noisy = np.where(mask, noisy, 0.0)
        return noisy.astype(obs.dtype)

# ... (truncated for brevity)
Combining Mitigation Strategies

No single mitigation technique closes the reality gap for all tasks. In practice, a layered pipeline is most effective. Phase 1 is hardware measurement: run SysID experiments before any RL training to identify key parameters. Phase 2 is grounded domain randomisation: use measured values as distribution centres and add observation noise plus action delay wrappers. Phase 3 is optional residual learning: if a test deployment reveals systematic bias (the robot consistently overshoots in a particular direction), collect real rollouts, train a residual net on the error, and re-run RL training with the residual wrapper inserted. Phase 4 is deployment adaptation: use MAML-initialised weights or an online MRAC layer to handle residual variation between units and over time. The combination of these layers delivers substantially better transfer than any individual component because each targets a different mechanism through which simulation diverges from reality.
Phase 1 — SysID: measure inertia, friction, motor delay before training
Phase 2 — Grounded DR: centre distributions on measured values; add noise/delay wrappers
Phase 3 — Residual learning: train correction net on real vs sim trajectory differences
Phase 4 — Deployment adaptation: MAML inner-loop or MRAC for unit-to-unit variation
Log sim success rate vs real success rate after each phase to track gap closure
Prioritise cheap mitigations (noise/delay injection) before expensive ones (residual data collection)
Measuring the Reality Gap

Before applying mitigation techniques, it is useful to quantify the current reality gap so that progress can be tracked. A simple metric is trajectory divergence: run the same policy in sim and on the real robot from identical initial conditions and compute the mean L2 norm between the corresponding state sequences, ||s_real(t) - s_sim(t)||, averaged over time and over multiple episodes. A larger divergence indicates a wider gap. A complementary metric is transfer success rate: the fraction of episodes that achieve task success in the real world, divided by the sim success rate. A ratio near 1.0 indicates complete transfer; a ratio near 0 indicates complete failure. For contact-rich tasks, it is informative to separate joint-space divergence from end-effector divergence, because errors compound differently along the kinematic chain.
Trajectory divergence: L2(s_real(t), s_sim(t)) over matched episodes
Transfer ratio: real_success_rate / sim_success_rate
State distribution divergence: Wasserstein distance between sim and real state marginals
Phase-space mismatch: plot joint position vs velocity; compare sim and real Lissajous figures
Contact event timing: compare timestamps of ground contact events between sim and real
Energy budget: total joint power in sim vs real reveals unmodelled dissipation
Practical Workflow

For a new robot and task, the recommended workflow is: (1) generate a URDF/MJCF from CAD and set nominal physics parameters; (2) run SysID experiments on the real hardware using chirp and step responses, update the simulation parameters; (3) add observation noise, action delay, and broad domain randomisation wrappers; (4) train the RL policy to convergence in the grounded, randomised sim; (5) deploy on the real robot and measure the trajectory divergence and transfer success rate; (6) if the gap is still too large, collect 5000–10 000 real transitions and train a residual network; (7) re-train the policy with the residual wrapper; (8) optionally, wrap the final deployed policy with an MRAC layer for online adaptation to unit-to-unit variation. Each step has a measurable cost and a measurable benefit, making it possible to decide when further investment in gap-closing is not worth the marginal gain in transfer success.
Start with CAD-derived nominal parameters rather than default simulator values
Always measure motor delay empirically; it varies by driver firmware version
Use ROS bag replay to align real and simulated trajectories for residual computation
Residual nets trained on 5000 transitions generalise reasonably; 20 000 is more reliable
MRAC is most useful for tasks with a clearly identified scalar uncertainty (payload mass)
MAML inner-loop adaptation works best when k <= 10 real episodes are feasible before deployment
Version-control SysID data alongside model checkpoints for reproducible training pipelines
Summary

The reality gap arises from three interlocking sources: unmodeled physics (contact, compliance, wear), sensor imperfection (noise, latency, quantisation), and actuator nonlinearity (delay, stiction, thermal effects). System identification closes the modelling component by measuring and fitting real hardware parameters. Residual physics learning handles structurally absent phenomena by learning the correction term from real rollouts. Adaptive control updates gain parameters online as conditions change. Meta-learning provides an initial policy configuration that adapts in few steps. Grounded simulation combines SysID with domain randomisation for principled parameter distributions. Noise and delay injection hardens the policy against known imperfections at negligible cost. Used together, these techniques make it possible to train complex manipulation and locomotion policies entirely in simulation and deploy them on the first or second real-world trial.
